prior.title: UNCTAD Transport Infrastructure and Services Programme: Global Guidance on Port Resilience and Sustainable Freight Transport
prior.summary: UNCTAD's Transport Infrastructure and Services work programme provides guidance and technical assistance to developing countries for integrating into global trade through efficient, climate-friendly, and socially inclusive freight transport systems. Key outputs include the Guidebook for Ports featuring risk management and resilience-building tools, and port management monographs offering practical experiences for capacity development in port operations.
prior.full_brief:
# UNCTAD Transport Infrastructure and Services Programme: Global Guidance on Port Resilience and Sustainable Freight Transport

**Format:** Research Summary
**Severity:** MONITORING
**Programme Status:** Active, multi-output — guidebook published (August 2022), training course live, SFT project completed (2018), SDG monitoring ongoing (2025 edition active)
**Re-check window:** Review when UNCTAD Review of Maritime Transport 2025 full edition analysis is available, and ahead of any announced 12th session Multi-year Expert Meeting outputs (next session: 13–14 April 2026, Geneva)

---

## Section 1 — What the Research Found

MONITORING

**Programme scope and architecture.** The UNCTAD Transport Infrastructure and Services Programme describes its work programme as supporting "developing countries in their effective integration into global and regional trade and value chains through efficient, reliable, cost-effective, environmentally friendly, climate-friendly, low-carbon, and socially inclusive freight transport systems," with three priority action areas: sustainable freight transport, corridor performance, and maritime transport. *Source: UNCTAD Transport Infrastructure and Services, UNCTAD, [undated page, accessed 2026]. https://unctad.org/topic/transport-and-trade-logistics/infrastructure-and-services.

**Port Resilience Guidebook — published finding.** The *Building Capacity to Manage Risks and Enhance Resilience: A Guidebook for Ports* (document reference UNCTAD/TCS/DTL/INF/2022/3, published 1 August 2022) "presents a step-by-step approach to resilience building in the maritime supply chain" and "sets out risk identification, assessment and management tools and techniques, and describes a resilience-building process for ports." *Source: Building Capacity to Manage Risks and Enhance Resilience: A Guidebook for Ports, UNCTAD, 1 August 2022. https://unctad.org/publication/building-capacity-manage-risks-and-enhance-resilience-guidebook-ports.

The guidebook identifies three categories of mitigation and response measures: (1) before a disruption materialises — "strategies that aim to anticipate, plan, prepare, forecast, and integrate uncertainty through scenario planning, as well as invest in data and intelligence gathering for greater preparedness"; (2) during a disruption — "protocols and emergency responses"; and (3) after a disruption — "actions that seek to mitigate impact, enable recovery and ensure adaptation to an emerging 'new normal'." *Source: Building Capacity to Manage Risks and Enhance Resilience: A Guidebook for Ports, UNCTAD, 1 August 2022. https://unctad.org/publication/building-capacity-manage-risks-and-enhance-resilience-guidebook-ports.

**Stakeholder scope.** The guidebook identifies its target audience as "stakeholders across the maritime supply chain involved in building port resilience," specifically naming: governmental planning and regulatory agencies; port authorities; port operators and port management companies; terminal operators; infrastructure managers; freight forwarders; customs authorities; carriers and shipping companies; shippers and cargo owners; and inland carriers and inland logistics operators. *Source: Building Capacity to Manage Risks and Enhance Resilience: A Guidebook for Ports, UNCTAD, 1 August 2022. https://unctad.org/publication/building-capacity-manage-risks-and-enhance-resilience-guidebook-ports.

**Container focus and value rationale.** The guidance states that "although containerized shipments only represent about 16 percent of world maritime trade by volume, they account for more than 70 percent of its value," and that "container shipping and ports are particularly relevant to commercial and retail supply chains on which consumers directly depend upon," making ports "potential single points of failure in the maritime supply chain." *Source: Building Capacity to Manage Risks and Enhance Resilience: A Guidebook for Ports, UNCTAD, 1 August 2022. https://unctad.org/publication/building-capacity-manage-risks-and-enhance-resilience-guidebook-ports.

**Online training platform.** The Resilient Maritime Logistics website describes the *UNCTAD Course on Ports and Maritime Supply Chain Resilience* (4 July 2022) as containing "six modules" and being "supported by the accompanying multimedia lectures and suggested additional reading," drawing heavily on the Guidebook, the *Review of Maritime Transport*, and "relevant analytical reports and statistics in the field of transport and trade logistics." *Source: Resilient Maritime Logistics — UNCTAD, UNCTAD, 4 July 2022. https://resilientmaritimelogistics.unctad.org/.

**Sustainable Freight Transport (SFT) capacity-building project — completed.** UNCTAD implemented a project titled "Building the Capacities of Developing Countries to Shift Towards Sustainable Freight Transport" under the 9th Tranche of the UN Development Account, with project code 1415Q, duration 2014–2018, and a budget of $611,000. The project was "aimed at strengthening the capacity of policy makers (in transport, infrastructure and finance), transport operators (in rail, road, and ports from public and private sector), and key financial institutions, in East Africa and the Caribbean." Outputs include the UNCTAD Sustainable Freight Transport Framework and the UNCTAD Sustainable Freight Transport and Finance Training Toolkit. *Source: Building capacities of developing countries to shift towards sustainable freight transport, UNCTAD, [project page]. https://unctad.org/project/building-capacities-developing-countries-shift-towards-sustainable-freight-transport.

**SFT toolkit components.** The SFT tools include: "an SFT framework providing step-by-step guidance to plan, design, develop, and implement tailored SFT strategies"; "two SFT tools to facilitate the implementation of SFT framework, including key performance indicators and SFT measures catalogue"; a "Land Freight Emission Model to calculate air pollutant emissions and carbon emissions from land freight transport"; and a training toolkit including modules on "Improving the Environmental Performance of Freight Transport" and "Public Private Partnerships (PPPs) for Sustainable Freight Transport Infrastructure and Logistics." *Source: UNCTAD Transport Infrastructure and Services, UNCTAD, [undated page, accessed 2026]. https://unctad.org/topic/transport-and-trade-logistics/infrastructure-and-services.

**Chokepoint disruption data (Review of Maritime Transport 2024).** Global maritime trade grew by 2.4% to 12.3 billion tons in 2023, with the sector projected to grow by 2% in 2024 and an average of 2.4% annually through 2029. By mid-2024, ship capacity crossing the Gulf of Aden was "down 76% and tonnage transiting the Suez Canal cut by 70%," with Cape of Good Hope arrivals surging 89%. Longer routes raised "global vessel ton-mile demand by 3% and container ship demand by 12%." For a 20,000–24,000 TEU vessel on the Far East–Europe route, "CO2 emissions alone add $400,000 in costs under the European Union's Emissions Trading System." *Source: Review of Maritime Transport 2024, UNCTAD, 22 October 2024. https://unctad.org/publication/review-maritime-transport-2024.

**Freight rate data.** The Shanghai Containerized Freight Index (SCFI) "had more than doubled from late 2023" by mid-2024. The SCFI Shanghai–South America route average rate rose to $9,026 per TEU (January–July 2024), "the highest level since September 2022." The SCFI Shanghai–South Africa route average "almost tripled to $5,426 per TEU," and the Shanghai–West Africa average "jumped 137% to $5,563 per TEU." UNCTAD projected freight cost surges would push global consumer prices up by 0.6% by 2025, with small island developing States (SIDS) facing a 0.9% consumer price increase and processed food costs rising 1.3%. *Source: Review of Maritime Transport 2024, UNCTAD, 22 October 2024. https://unctad.org/publication/review-maritime-transport-2024.

**Climate risk to port infrastructure.** UNCTAD SDG Pulse 2025 states that "weather and climate-driven extreme events pose increasing risks for global ports, making adaptation, resilience-building and disaster risk reduction an urgent imperative." Extreme sea levels of a magnitude previously expected to occur once a century are projected to occur "much more often under climate change, significantly increasing the flood hazard for global ports, including some of the 100 largest container ports." Ports in SIDS "are particularly vulnerable and lack adaptive capacity," with assets "projected to be at high and increasing risk of coastal flooding, from as early as the 2030s." *Source: UNCTAD SDG Pulse 2025 — Transport Resilience and Sustainability, UNCTAD, 2025. https://sdgpulse.unctad.org/sustainable-transport/.

**IAPH collaboration.** UNCTAD produces port management monographs "in collaboration with the International Association of Ports and Harbors (IAPH)," described as "a series of technical studies in the form of monographs prepared in collaboration with the International Association of Ports and Harbors (IAPH), presenting practical experiences gained by a specific port or professionals for the benefit of the international port community." IAPH "has consultative status and works on behalf of ports with additional United Nations bodies such as the UNCTAD." *Sources: UNCTAD Transport Infrastructure and Services, UNCTAD, [undated]. https://unctad.org/topic/transport-and-trade-logistics/infrastructure-and-services; About IAPH, IAPH, [undated, data as of 6 December 2024]. https://www.iaphworldports.org/about-iaph/.

**SDG 9 alignment.** UNCTAD SDG Pulse 2025 links the programme's outputs to SDG 9, Target 9.1: "Develop quality, reliable, sustainable and resilient infrastructure, including regional and transborder infrastructure, to support economic development and human well-being." The platform's framing states that "transport infrastructure and services facilitate trade, support global supply chains, propel growth, and promote social progress." *Source: UNCTAD SDG Pulse 2025 — Transport Resilience and Sustainability, UNCTAD, 2025. https://sdgpulse.unctad.org/sustainable-transport/.

---

## Section 2 — Why This Finding Matters Operationally and Commercially

**For workspaces operating as freight forwarders across ocean, air, and road modes:**

*Operational implication:* The UNCTAD guidebook explicitly names freight forwarders as a target stakeholder group for port resilience planning, placing them in the same stakeholder ecosystem as port authorities, terminal operators, customs authorities, carriers, and shippers. This is a programme designed partly for the workspace's role — not a general-policy document with incidental relevance.

*Operational implication:* The chokepoint disruption data in the Review of Maritime Transport 2024 directly prices the cost of non-resilience for ocean freight operations: a 70% reduction in Suez Canal throughput and a 76% drop in Gulf of Aden traffic are not projections — they are documented 2024 figures that represent the operating baseline for trade lanes across the Americas–Europe and Asia–Europe corridors that the workspace actively uses. Rerouting costs, EU ETS carbon surcharges (documented at $400,000 additional per Cape-rerouted ultra-large vessel), and doubled freight rates on South America and West Africa routes are the mechanical consequence of the resilience gap the programme addresses.

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces moving time-sensitive cargo by air (live events, fine art, film/TV, luxury goods, humanitarian) where ocean is tertiary, the primary operational relevance of port resilience guidance lies in origin/destination port congestion affecting intermodal dwell time and in connecting surface freight legs — not in vessel rerouting per se. However, the documented 89% surge in Cape of Good Hope arrivals and associated congestion patterns affect cargo throughput times at transshipment hubs that connect with air freight gateways, particularly in Africa and the Indian Ocean region.

*Operational implication:* The UNCTAD Land Freight Emission Model, as a component of the SFT toolkit, is relevant to workspaces with road freight operations and EU Scope 3 reporting obligations. Where the workspace is subject to CSRD or client-driven emissions disclosure, the model provides a recognised UN-methodology baseline for land freight emissions calculation — a defensible input for sustainability reporting.

*Analytical inference:* The SIDS connectivity finding — a 9% drop in shipping connectivity over the past decade and a position "ten times less connected than the rest of the world" — is a direct risk signal for workspaces operating humanitarian cargo lanes to island territories. Reduced connectivity in these markets increases schedule unreliability, reduces carrier choice, and widens the gap between ocean and air freight cost trade-offs for humanitarian shipments.

**EU ETS intersection — COST ALERT.** The Review of Maritime Transport 2024 specifically quantifies EU ETS costs on rerouted voyages: CO2 costs alone add $400,000 per ultra-large vessel on the Far East–Europe route under Cape rerouting. *Source: Review of Maritime Transport 2024, UNCTAD, 22 October 2024. https://unctad.org/publication/review-maritime-transport-2024. *Operational implication:* For workspaces moving ocean freight on Far East–Europe lanes, EU ETS surcharges are already flowing through carrier rate cards; this figure provides a documented reference point for validating the scale of carrier ETS pass-through claims against verified cost structures.

---

## Section 3 — What the Finding Changes for Strategy, Claims, or Decisions

*Analytical inference:* The three-phase resilience framework (before/during/after disruption) in the UNCTAD Guidebook provides a structured basis for the workspace to audit its own contingency protocols against an internationally recognised port-resilience standard — not as a regulatory compliance requirement, but as a credible operational framework from a Tier 3 intergovernmental source. Workspaces that can demonstrate alignment with the guidebook's pre-disruption scanning and scenario planning components are better positioned in client conversations about supply-chain continuity, particularly for high-value cargo verticals where clients carry high exposure to single points of failure.

*Analytical inference:* The SFT project's completed PPP financing module and toolkit outputs (2014–2018) represent available reference material for workspaces advising clients on sustainable freight infrastructure investment in East Africa and Caribbean markets. These are not current advisory engagements; the project is closed. But the materials remain publicly available and provide a UN-endorsed framing for conversations about sustainable freight strategies in those corridors.

**Climate risk and the 2030s horizon.** The SDG Pulse 2025 data on coastal flooding risk to port infrastructure — with assets in SIDS at high risk "from as early as the 2030s" — changes the planning horizon for any workspace holding long-term operational commitments (infrastructure, warehousing, port partnerships) in vulnerable island markets. *Operational implication:* This is a medium-term strategic risk signal, not an immediate compliance action. However, workspaces advising humanitarian clients or managing long-term contracts in SIDS markets should log this as a location-risk factor in their lane assessments.

*Analytical inference:* The container port throughput data — 858.2 million TEU in 2023, 217 million TEU above the decade-earlier level — confirms continued volume growth as the structural baseline. Workspaces that position themselves to operate efficiently through resilience disruptions (pre-cleared relationships with alternative transshipment hubs, pre-negotiated rerouting protocols, contingency carrier agreements) are advantaged relative to those that manage disruptions reactively.

**Pending regulatory intersection — IMO decarbonisation.** The Review of Maritime Transport 2024 flags the aging global fleet and slow decarbonisation as a structural constraint, noting that "high costs, uncertainty over future fuels and low ship scrapping rates" are retarding fleet renewal. *Analytical inference:* IMO's 2023 GHG Strategy targets (net-zero by or around 2050, with interim 2030 and 2040 checkpoints) are the regulatory driver behind the fleet-renewal pressure that UNCTAD is monitoring. For workspaces with ocean freight exposure, the intersection of fleet ageing, fuel uncertainty, and EU ETS cost escalation is the most commercially consequential near-term dynamic from this programme's monitoring output.

*Specific regulation text, IMO 2023 GHG Strategy, not available from primary sources as of 2026-05-28.*

---

## Section 4 — Client Conversation Talking Points and Public Position

**What the workspace can credibly say:**

1. UNCTAD's 2024 maritime transport data documents a 70% drop in Suez Canal throughput and a doubling of freight rates on multiple routes by mid-2024 — these are the verified operating conditions the workspace is navigating, not projections. This supports factual conversations about surcharge drivers and route changes without overclaiming.

2. The UNCTAD Guidebook identifies freight forwarders as a named stakeholder in port resilience planning alongside port authorities and terminal operators. The workspace can reference this positioning — the UN intergovernmental body recognises forwarders as a resilience actor, not just a passive service buyer — when discussing continuity planning with clients.

3. For clients asking about EU ETS surcharges on ocean freight, the UNCTAD-documented $400,000 additional CO2 cost per ultra-large vessel (Far East–Europe, Cape routing) provides a credible external reference point for why carrier ETS surcharges exist and at what order of magnitude — without the workspace having to endorse any specific carrier's surcharge calculation.

4. For humanitarian clients or clients with SIDS supply chains, the SDG Pulse 2025 finding that SIDS ports are projected to face high coastal flooding risk from the 2030s is a factual, UNCTAD-sourced basis for discussing lane resilience and alternative routing.

**Pitfalls to avoid:**

- *Analytical inference:* Do not cite the SFT project (2014–2018) as a current active programme — it is completed. The toolkit outputs are available but the advisory project has concluded. The ongoing elements are the Guidebook, the online training platform, the SDG monitoring, and the annual Review of Maritime Transport.
- Do not present the Guidebook's three-phase resilience framework as a regulatory standard or binding compliance requirement. It is intergovernmental guidance — a Tier 3 source by this platform's hierarchy — not a binding regulation. The workspace can reference it as a recognised framework; counsel confirmation would be needed before using it as a contractual compliance benchmark.
- The freight rate projections (0.6% global consumer price rise by 2025 from shipping costs) and regional variants are UNCTAD estimates published in October 2024 and labelled as projections. Present them as projections, not confirmed outcomes, in client conversations.

---

## Section 5 — What the Finding Does Not Resolve

This brief does NOT resolve whether any of the UNCTAD programme's toolkit outputs (SFT Framework, Land Freight Emission Model, Port Risk Management Toolbox) satisfy specific EU regulatory reporting requirements (CSRD Scope 3, EU ETS MRV) — *Legal Confirmation Required:* alignment with EU regulatory methodologies requires legal and technical review against the applicable regulatory text before the workspace relies on UNCTAD tools for statutory disclosure.

The brief does NOT resolve the current status of the Review of Maritime Transport 2025 (published 24 September 2025 per the programme page, but full content not available in the source blocks). The 2025 edition may update the 2024 chokepoint data and freight rate projections materially. *Specific figures from RMT 2025 not available from primary sources as of 2026-05-28.*

The brief does NOT resolve UNCTAD's current pipeline of SFT country assessments beyond the Angola assessment (13 January 2025) listed in the programme publications. Whether additional East Africa or Caribbean assessments are underway or planned is not stated in the available sources.

The brief does NOT resolve the outcome of the 12th session of the Multi-year Expert Meeting on transport, trade logistics and trade facilitation (scheduled 13–14 April 2026). *Analytical inference:* This meeting is the primary intergovernmental venue where UNCTAD's transport programme guidance is validated and mandated by member states; its outputs would be the relevant policy signal to monitor for any change in programme direction or new guidance mandates.

---
